items.question_text: What does a through clearance authorize?

choices.text: Clearing an aircraft through intermediate stops.
Cancelling IFR at each intermediate stop and reissuing it after departure.
Landing only at the final destination shown in the clearance.
Bypassing every airport between departure and destination without stopping.

items.explanation: Paragraph 4-2-6 allows an aircraft to be cleared through intermediate stops.

items.situation: An IFR aircraft will stop at O'Hare and then continue to Joliet VOR on the same clearance. Build the through clearance.
items.target_phrase: CLEARED THROUGH OHARE TO JOLIET V O R

items.instruction: Which transmission correctly states a through clearance?
items.question_text: Which phrase is correct?

choices.text: Cleared through O'Hare to Joliet V O R.
Cleared to O'Hare through Joliet V O R.
Through O'Hare, cleared Joliet V O R.
Proceed through O'Hare then contact Joliet V O R.

items.explanation: The approved structure is CLEARED THROUGH (airport) TO (fix).

items.instruction: The controller issued this clearance. Which pilot readback is correct?
items.clearance: CLEARED THROUGH OHARE TO JOLIET V O R.

choices.text: Cleared through O'Hare to Joliet V O R, Cessna Three Alpha Bravo.
Cleared to O'Hare, then Joliet V O R, Cessna Three Alpha Bravo.
Roger, Cessna Three Alpha Bravo.
Cleared through Joliet V O R to O'Hare, Cessna Three Alpha Bravo.

items.explanation: A correct readback preserves the through-clearance structure and identifies the aircraft.

items.question_text: What phrase is used when an aircraft will operate in an approved ALTRV?

choices.text: Via approved altitude reservation flight plan.
Via military route reservation as filed.
Via special use airspace flight plan.
Proceed via reserved altitude block.

items.explanation: Paragraph 4-2-7 gives the exact phrase VIA APPROVED ALTITUDE RESERVATION (mission name) FLIGHT PLAN.

items.situation: A military aircraft will operate in an approved ALTRV mission named Cobra. Build the ALTRV portion of the clearance.
items.target_phrase: VIA APPROVED ALTITUDE RESERVATION COBRA FLIGHT PLAN

items.instruction: Does this match when the ALTRV phrase is used?
items.question_text: This phrase is used when the aircraft will operate in an approved ALTRV.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The phrase is inserted when the aircraft will operate in an approved altitude reservation.

items.instruction: Which description of normal ALTRV coverage is correct?
items.question_text: Which statement is correct?

choices.text: It normally includes departure, climb, cruise, and arrival up to the holding pattern or the point or time ATC provides separation.
It normally covers only cruise flight after top of climb and ends automatically at top of descent.
It applies only after the aircraft has entered special use airspace.
It ends at the boundary of the departure controller's airspace regardless of mission phase.

items.explanation: The note says ALTRV normally includes departure, climb, cruise, and arrival up to the holding pattern or the point or time ATC provides separation.

items.situation: A military aircraft will operate in an approved ALTRV mission. Another controller says the route and altitude already show the reservation, so there is no need to say anything special in the clearance.
items.para_context: When the aircraft will operate in an approved ALTRV, use the phrase VIA APPROVED ALTITUDE RESERVATION (mission name) FLIGHT PLAN.

choices.text: Include the approved ALTRV phrase in the clearance.
Omit the phrase because ALTRV handling is implied whenever the flight is military.
Use an as filed route and tell the pilot to confirm the reservation after departure.
Replace the phrase with proceed via reserved block altitude.

items.explanation: The paragraph requires the ALTRV phrase when the aircraft will operate in an approved altitude reservation.

items.question_text: If a flight will operate IFR first and then continue VFR, where should it be cleared?

choices.text: To the fix where the IFR part of the flight ends.
Only to the departure airport's overlying approach control area.
Directly to the final destination under IFR regardless of where VFR begins.
To the first point where radar service will be terminated.

items.explanation: IFR-then-VFR flights are cleared to the fix where IFR ends.

items.instruction: Does this application statement match the rule?
items.question_text: This VFR-departure handling applies to a flight that will operate VFR first and then IFR.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Paragraph 4-2-8 applies that handling to flights that will depart VFR and begin IFR later.

items.instruction: Which added step is required for a Mode C aircraft changing from VFR to IFR?
items.question_text: Which action is correct?

choices.text: Assign a beacon code that will allow MSAW alarms.
Keep the existing VFR code until the aircraft reaches the MEA.
Delay code assignment until the aircraft is already established on an IFR route.
Assign a code only if the pilot specifically requests radar service.

items.explanation: When changing from VFR to IFR, assign a Mode C aircraft a beacon code that will allow MSAW alarms.

items.situation: A VFR aircraft below the minimum IFR altitude requests a pop-up IFR clearance. Before issuing it, you must ask whether the pilot can maintain terrain and obstruction clearance until reaching the minimum vectoring altitude. Build the question.
items.target_phrase: NOVEMBER ONE TWO THREE ALFA BRAVO ARE YOU ABLE TO MAINTAIN YOUR OWN TERRAIN AND OBSTRUCTION CLEARANCE UNTIL REACHING MINIMUM VECTORING ALTITUDE

items.situation: A Mode C VFR aircraft below the minimum IFR altitude requests an IFR clearance. The pilot says they can maintain their own terrain and obstruction clearance until reaching the minimum vectoring altitude. Another controller tells you to keep the aircraft VFR until it climbs on its own to the MEA because pop-up IFR cannot be issued below minimum IFR altitude.
items.para_context: If the pilot can maintain terrain and obstruction clearance until reaching the appropriate minimum IFR altitude, issue the IFR clearance. Mode C aircraft changing from VFR to IFR should receive a beacon code that will allow MSAW alarms.

choices.text: Issue the IFR clearance and handle the beacon code so MSAW alarms will function.
Refuse the IFR clearance because pop-up IFR can never be issued below minimum IFR altitude.
Vector the aircraft below the minimum IFR altitude until it reaches the airway.
Keep the aircraft on the VFR code until it is established above the MEA, then issue IFR.

items.explanation: If the pilot can maintain terrain and obstruction clearance until the proper minimum altitude, the IFR clearance may be issued.

items.question_text: Do not assign or imply specific course guidance below the MIA or MEA, even if the pilot accepts responsibility for terrain and obstruction clearance.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Controllers should not assign or imply specific course guidance below the MIA or MEA in this pop-up IFR case.

items.question_text: You must instruct the pilot to maintain VFR and state intentions if the pilot cannot maintain terrain and obstruction clearance until reaching the appropriate minimum IFR altitude.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: If the pilot cannot maintain terrain and obstruction clearance to the proper minimum, the pilot is told to maintain VFR and state intentions.

items.question_text: Before processing an airfile request, what must you verify unless other coordination already exists?

choices.text: That the aircraft is within your area of jurisdiction.
That the aircraft has already been radar identified by the destination facility.
That the pilot has already received a complete destination clearance from flight service.
That the aircraft is above the MEA for the entire route of flight.

items.explanation: The first airfile guideline is to ensure the aircraft is within your jurisdiction unless otherwise coordinated.

items.instruction: Put the airfile handling steps in order.

steps.text: Ensure the aircraft is within your area of jurisdiction unless otherwise coordinated.
Obtain the information needed to provide IFR service.
Issue a destination clearance, short-range clearance, or an instruction to contact FSS if the flight plan cannot be processed.

items.explanation: Airfile handling begins with jurisdiction, then the needed IFR-service information, then the appropriate clearance or FSS instruction.

items.instruction: The controller issued this clearance. Which pilot readback is correct?

choices.text: Cleared to Tulsa airport, Cessna Three Alpha Bravo.
Cleared Tulsa, Cessna Three Alpha Bravo.
Proceed Tulsa airport, Cessna Three Alpha Bravo.
Cleared to destination Tulsa, Cessna Three Alpha Bravo.

items.explanation: If the airfile aircraft is cleared to the destination airport, the clearance uses CLEARED TO (destination) AIRPORT.

items.instruction: Which clearance correctly names the NAVAID when the type is known?
items.question_text: Which transmission is correct?

choices.text: Cleared to Gordonsville V O R.
Cleared to Gordonsville.
Cleared to V O R Gordonsville.
Cleared via Gordonsville airport.

items.explanation: If the clearance is to a NAVAID and the type is known, the type must follow the NAVAID name.

items.question_text: You must not treat airfile processing as having priority over another ATC duty.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: The note explicitly says the procedures do not imply airfile processing has priority over another ATC duty.

items.situation: An aircraft calls airborne requesting an airfile IFR clearance. You determine the flight plan cannot be processed right now. Another controller says to give a rough destination clearance anyway and tell the pilot the rest can be sorted out later.
items.para_context: If the flight plan cannot be processed, one of the listed options is to instruct the aircraft to contact flight service.

choices.text: Instruct the pilot to contact flight service.
Issue a destination clearance anyway and fix the flight plan after handoff.
Tell the pilot to stay VFR and continue without any additional instruction.
Delay all action until another controller can take the request.

items.explanation: If the flight plan cannot be processed, instructing the aircraft to contact flight service is one of the approved outcomes.

items.question_text: Before telling an arriving IFR aircraft to change to CTAF at an airport without a tower or FSS, what must you do if needed?

choices.text: Provide instructions on how to cancel the IFR flight plan.
Tell the pilot IFR cancellation is automatic after touchdown in every case.
Keep the aircraft on your frequency until the engine is shut down.
Transfer the cancellation responsibility to the next ATC facility automatically.

items.explanation: If needed, cancellation instructions are provided before telling the pilot to change to CTAF.

items.instruction: Which transmission is correct for an airport with an air/ground communications station?
items.question_text: Which phrase is correct?

choices.text: Report cancellation of IFR on 122.4.
Report cancellation of IFR this frequency and with flight service.
Cancel IFR automatically on CTAF.
Advise ground when IFR is canceled.

items.explanation: With an air/ground communications station, the instruction is REPORT CANCELLATION OF IFR ON (frequency).

items.instruction: One word in this phraseology is incorrect. Tap it.
items.original_phrase: REPORT CANCELLATION OF IFR THIS FREQUENCY OR WITH FLIGHT SERVICE.
items.display_text: REPORT CANCELLATION OF IFR THIS FREQUENCY AND WITH FLIGHT SERVICE
items.explanation: Without an air/ground communications station, the phrase uses OR: report cancellation of IFR this frequency or with flight service.

items.situation: An aircraft is arriving IFR at an airport without an air/ground communications station. You want it to report cancellation of IFR this frequency or with McAlester Radio. Build the instruction.
items.target_phrase: NOVEMBER ONE THREE WHISKEY ALFA REPORT CANCELLATION OF IFR THIS FREQUENCY OR WITH MCALESTER RADIO

items.situation: An arriving IFR aircraft reports cancellation on your frequency after landing at an airport without a tower. Another controller says no response is necessary because the pilot already knows the cancellation went through when no conflict alert appears.
items.para_context: When the cancellation is received, acknowledge it with the phrase IFR CANCELLATION RECEIVED.

choices.text: Acknowledge the report with IFR cancellation received.
Say nothing further because the pilot initiated the cancellation.
Instruct the pilot to call back on CTAF with the cancellation.
Tell the pilot to contact flight service for acknowledgment instead of giving one yourself.

items.explanation: When the cancellation is received, respond with IFR CANCELLATION RECEIVED.

items.question_text: At an airport without an air/ground communications station, you may instruct the pilot to report cancellation of IFR this frequency or with flight service.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: That is one of the approved cancellation-reporting instructions when there is no air/ground communications station.

items.question_text: What does a through clearance authorize?
items.explanation: Paragraph 4-2-6 authorizes clearing an aircraft through intermediate stops.

choices.text: Clearing an aircraft through intermediate stops.
Cancelling IFR at each stop and reissuing it later.
Prohibiting any intermediate stop after departure.
Restricting the aircraft to one landing only.

items.question_text: Which transmission correctly issues a through clearance?
items.explanation: The approved structure is CLEARED THROUGH (airport) TO (fix).

choices.text: Cleared through O'Hare to Joliet V O R.
Cleared to O'Hare through Joliet V O R.
Proceed through O'Hare then Joliet V O R.
Cleared through to O'Hare Joliet V O R.

items.question_text: In through-clearance phraseology, what word comes immediately before the intermediate-stop airport?
items.explanation: The airport follows the word THROUGH in the phrase CLEARED THROUGH (airport) TO (fix).

choices.text: Through.
Via.
Over.
Past.

items.question_text: In the phrase CLEARED THROUGH (airport) TO (fix), what follows the word to?
items.explanation: The final fix follows the word TO.

choices.text: The final fix.
The departure airport.
The approach frequency.
The aircraft call sign.

items.question_text: What phrase is used when an aircraft will operate in an approved ALTRV?
items.explanation: The phrase is VIA APPROVED ALTITUDE RESERVATION (mission name) FLIGHT PLAN.

choices.text: Via approved altitude reservation flight plan.
Via military route reservation as filed.
Proceed via special reservation corridor.
As filed in altitude reservation remarks.

items.question_text: When is the ALTRV phrase inserted into the clearance?
items.explanation: It is used when the aircraft will operate in an approved altitude reservation.

choices.text: When the aircraft will operate in an approved ALTRV.
Whenever the aircraft is military, regardless of route type.
Only after the aircraft has entered cruise flight.
Only if the aircraft is not receiving radar service.

items.question_text: Which phases are normally included in an approved ALTRV?
items.explanation: The note says ALTRV normally includes departure, climb, cruise, and arrival.

choices.text: Departure, climb, cruise, and arrival.
Cruise only.
Arrival only after the holding pattern is reached.
Ground movement, departure, and cruise only.

items.question_text: Normal ALTRV coverage usually extends up to what point?
items.explanation: It normally extends to the holding pattern or to the point or time ATC provides separation.

choices.text: The holding pattern or the point or time ATC provides separation.
The top of climb only.
The boundary of the departure controller's airspace.
The moment the aircraft checks in with center.

items.question_text: Which transmission correctly contains the ALTRV phrase?
items.explanation: The mission name is inserted into the phrase VIA APPROVED ALTITUDE RESERVATION (mission name) FLIGHT PLAN.

choices.text: Via approved altitude reservation Cobra flight plan.
Via approved Cobra altitude reservation plan.
Cobra mission via approved reserved altitude.
Approved altitude reservation via Cobra route plan.

items.question_text: If a flight will operate IFR first and then continue VFR, where should it be cleared?
items.explanation: IFR-then-VFR flights are cleared to the fix where IFR ends.

choices.text: To the fix where the IFR part of the flight ends.
To the final destination regardless of where VFR begins.
Only to the departure controller's boundary.
To the first radar handoff point.

items.question_text: How is a VFR-then-IFR flight treated at departure?
items.explanation: It is treated as a VFR departure.

choices.text: As a VFR departure.
As an IFR departure from brake release.
As a special VFR departure only.
As uncontrolled flight until center accepts the strip.

items.question_text: What should be assigned to a Mode C aircraft changing from VFR to IFR?
items.explanation: Assign a beacon code that will allow MSAW alarms.

choices.text: A beacon code that will allow MSAW alarms.
The same VFR code until established on the airway.
A code only after the aircraft reaches the MEA.
No code change unless the pilot requests radar vectors.

items.question_text: What must ATC ask a below-minimum-altitude pilot requesting IFR if the aircraft cannot climb in VFR to the minimum IFR altitude?
items.explanation: ATC asks whether the pilot can maintain terrain and obstruction clearance until reaching the appropriate minimum altitude.

choices.text: Whether the pilot can maintain terrain and obstruction clearance until reaching the appropriate minimum altitude.
Whether the pilot can accept immediate vectors below the MIA.
Whether the pilot can climb at 500 feet per minute.
Whether the pilot can cancel IFR after departure if terrain becomes a factor.

items.question_text: If the pilot can maintain terrain and obstruction clearance until reaching the appropriate minimum IFR altitude, what may ATC issue?
items.explanation: If the pilot can maintain clearance to the proper minimum altitude, the IFR clearance may be issued.

choices.text: The IFR clearance.
Only a suggested heading until the pilot reaches the MEA.
Only flight-following service while remaining VFR.
A special VFR clearance instead of IFR.

items.question_text: If the pilot cannot maintain terrain and obstruction clearance until reaching the appropriate minimum IFR altitude, what should ATC instruct?
items.explanation: If the pilot cannot maintain that clearance, instruct the pilot to maintain VFR and state intentions.

choices.text: Maintain VFR and state intentions.
Turn direct to the nearest airway and climb as able.
Hold present heading and expect vectors below the MIA.
Squawk emergency and continue the climb at pilot discretion.

items.question_text: What should ATC not assign or imply below the MIA or MEA in this pop-up IFR situation?
items.explanation: The note says not to assign or imply specific course guidance below the MIA or MEA.

choices.text: Specific course guidance.
A beacon code.
An acknowledgment of the pop-up request.
A reminder to state intentions.

items.question_text: Before processing an airfile request, what must you verify unless other coordination already exists?
items.explanation: The aircraft must be within your area of jurisdiction unless otherwise coordinated.

choices.text: That the aircraft is within your area of jurisdiction.
That the destination controller has already approved the route.
That the aircraft is already above the MEA.
That the pilot has already contacted flight service.

items.question_text: What information should be obtained from an airfile aircraft after jurisdiction is confirmed?
items.explanation: Obtain the information needed to provide IFR service.

choices.text: The information needed to provide IFR service.
Only the destination airport and requested altitude.
Only the requested beacon code.
No additional information if the aircraft is radar identified.

items.question_text: If the flight plan cannot be processed, what is one approved outcome?
items.explanation: One approved outcome is to instruct the aircraft to contact flight service.

choices.text: Instruct the aircraft to contact flight service.
Issue an approximate route and correct it later.
Tell the pilot to continue IFR without a processed plan.
Delay action until the destination issues a release.

items.question_text: If you clear the airfile aircraft to the destination airport, what phrase should be used?
items.explanation: The phrase is CLEARED TO (destination) AIRPORT.

choices.text: Cleared to (destination) airport.
Cleared destination as filed.
Proceed to destination field.
Destination airport approved.

items.question_text: If a clearance is to a NAVAID and the type is known, what must follow the name?
items.explanation: The NAVAID type follows the name when it is known.

choices.text: The type of NAVAID.
The nearest airport name.
The frequency only.
The word airport.

items.question_text: What do the airfile procedures not imply?
items.explanation: The note says they do not imply airfile processing has priority over another ATC duty.

choices.text: That airfile processing has priority over another ATC duty.
That destination clearances may be issued to airfile aircraft.
That short-range clearances may be issued.
That airfile processing requires jurisdiction to be checked.

items.question_text: Before telling an arriving IFR aircraft to change to CTAF at an airport without a tower or FSS, what must you do if needed?
items.explanation: If needed, provide instructions on how to cancel the IFR flight plan before switching the pilot to CTAF.

choices.text: Provide instructions on how to cancel the IFR flight plan.
Tell the pilot to cancel automatically after landing.
Keep the pilot on approach frequency until shutdown.
Transfer the pilot to ground frequency for cancellation.

items.question_text: What instruction is used when the airport has an air/ground communications station?
items.explanation: The phrase is REPORT CANCELLATION OF IFR ON (frequency).

choices.text: Report cancellation of IFR on (frequency).
Report cancellation of IFR this frequency or with flight service.
Cancel IFR automatically on CTAF.
Contact tower after landing to cancel IFR.

items.question_text: At an airport without an air/ground communications station, what two reporting paths may be given in one approved instruction?
items.explanation: One approved instruction is to report cancellation of IFR this frequency or with flight service.

choices.text: This frequency or with flight service.
CTAF and then ground control.
The tower frequency and then center.
The approach frequency and then UNICOM only.

items.question_text: How should ATC respond when the IFR cancellation is received?
items.explanation: The acknowledgment phrase is IFR CANCELLATION RECEIVED.

choices.text: IFR cancellation received.
Cancellation understood, monitor CTAF.
IFR closed, contact flight service.
Roger, frequency change approved.

items.question_text: In the phrase REPORT CANCELLATION OF IFR THIS FREQUENCY OR WITH FLIGHT SERVICE, which word separates the two reporting options?
items.explanation: The correct word is OR.

choices.text: Or.
And.
Then.
After.

items.question_text: Which transmission is correct for an airport without an air/ground communications station?
items.explanation: Without an air/ground communications station, one approved form is REPORT CANCELLATION OF IFR THIS FREQUENCY OR WITH (FSS serving area or ATC controlling facility).

choices.text: Report cancellation of IFR this frequency or with McAlester Radio.
Report cancellation of IFR on McAlester Radio only.
Cancel IFR automatically, then call McAlester Radio if able.
Report IFR cancellation this frequency and with McAlester Radio.
